sentences.text: We arrived to Krakow, like I said.
We arrived to Krakow.
They told us there was in [NON-ENGLISH] 38 a place where Jewish get together now and-- on the bottom floor they said, we can't help you not so much, but my mother was very sick.
I repeat that bit.
And she had a big huge welt on the back of her neck, which they operated, and they cleaned it out.
She was very, very sick there.
For me, they told me to go on the third floor.
There was no greeting--
We just jumped the train before the train started-- stopping.
We walked.
We walked.
We didn't know where to go, but we spoke the language.
We spoke Polish.

We asked only where the address is, and they looked at us like crazy, but this is-- people didn't know what was coming or going because they didn't know it's Jews or not Jews because it was still fresh off the war.
And Germans we're not there, I don't think.
Maybe some Germans were hiding.
I wouldn't know.
There was just a mixture there, which there were Jews-- Jews finished.
So anybody else met on the street.
But we didn't pay any attention to nobody.
The only thing is, we saw somebody who looks like a Jew or anything like that-- [NON-ENGLISH].
If he answered that, all right.
If a Polish didn't know what it meant, nobody else knew what it meant.
We went to [NON-ENGLISH] 38.
Like I said, my mother went into the hospital.
I went on the third floor.
It was a big mess-- screaming and yelling, and this.
I met some guys there who were very rough and tough, and everything else, which they were-- a lot of them were in the partisans.
They were 15, 16.
If I wouldn't be-- having my mother to look after all the time, probably I would have been wind up in the partisans, but they lost everybody.
I was one or two, who had a mother.
No fathers.
If there was a father they didn't keep the child.
If there was only a mother-- and like my mother was very sick, so they kept me upstairs.
Because there was no other place to go.
The facilities-- I think there was four rooms, or five rooms in-- there was 25 or 30 people in one room.
And there was some straw-- the toilet was not there, and water was scarcely.
We didn't take showers to say.
They didn't give us no clothes to change.
Nobody had any clothes to wear.
Whatever we-- I had because I was wearing Russian uniforms.
So I had a few of them, which I saved up in that time.
So I had something on my clothes, I was hiding it-- nobody should take it away.
And all the time we used to have little children coming in to us.
Which they used to drop off because they were hiding out from Jewish families-- you know, from the-- the Christian.
They used to bring it in.
And all the time they were screaming and yelling, we are not Jews.
They wore a cross on them, and they used to pray in Polish.
And all the time-- the atmosphere was very bad.
The food that-- there was a woman that used to cook-- I don't know if she could cook enough.
There wasn't-- food.
I got a slice of bread and get something with it.
It was not-- nothing there.
We, as the older boys, we used to go out to the city.
Which we didn't have no money.
But to go on the streetcar-- we didn't pay.
When the conductor came, or something like that, we yelled at him.
We were not afraid.
There's one thing we learned after that, we were not afraid.
We were just on the way, we all planned to go to Israel and Palestine, and that's the only thing.
In our minds, in our soul, it's not Poland.
Just Palestine.
We went to movie houses-- we didn't pay-- because we didn't nowhere to pay.
And we went to a marketplace-- we took away from them.
The Polish used to scream.
We swiped some food.
We take away food.
We took all kinds-- we were rough.
Oh, yes.
We didn't take from nobody.
At that time if a Polish guy would say anything about a Jew, or anything like that-- so what do you want to make out of it?
Didn't kill enough?
Or anything like that.
A lot of times they said that we take away their children.
They start-- the incident [INAUDIBLE] children, which they came to us, it's true.
They came.
They wanted to take away-- they said that we took away Polish children, which they didn't-- we didn't.
We took Jewish children.
Who is they?
Polacks.
To the ground.
The ground was with a big fence-- steel fence.
And they came up to the grounds, and there was screaming and yelling.
We never gave in to them.
Yes, oh, yeah, all the time.
Because I kept with my mother-- I used to go down all the time, two, three times a day sometimes.
If I didn't go away with the boys, I used to go with her.
Oh, yeah.
In the hospital it was very bad.
It was very bad.
First-- it was bad because they didn't have no medicine.
They didn't have there-- didn't have nothing.
I don't know if there was a Jewish doctor or was a Polish doctor who came in there.
I really don't know who was the doctor there, who used to come in there.
But was not-- conditions were very bad down there.
It was too early in the-- of the war-- nothing was coming.
But later on, a woman came in, Helena Kuchler, and she got interested.
And she decided to take the children-- I don't know if she decided, or somebody decided-- it was organized.
It was not-- came out of the air.
But she couldn't do it on her own because we had a few trucks packed up.
And some of the children were weak, couldn't go to-- They rented a place, which is called [NON-ENGLISH], one of the most beautiful villas.
In [NON-ENGLISH] we had a three-story place.
In Zakopane.
And where is Zakopane?
Zakopane is in the middle of the mountains, and between the border of Czechoslovakia and Poland.
Zakopane means in Polish, buried.
Because why is it buried?
It's surrounded with mountains.
To get in there, the train goes through a tunnel.
Even horse and buggy goes through a tunnel.
There's no way in there, except through that tunnel.
And, like I said, a lot of the children could not go there because some of the doctors say, they cannot take the air.
Zakopane was very strong.
How many children were there at this time?
There was a lot-- a lot of children went through-- I wouldn't say how many because I don't know.
A lot.
It was more than a few hundred because what it is-- because all of a sudden there's a father found a child.
Came from a concentration camp, and took him away.
There's another one came, he took him away, that took away that one.
There's always somebody came and took away.
Nobody wanted to talk that the Jews were killed.
We knew that the liquidation of Krakow-- we went around Krakow.
Krakow was a Jewish city.
It was-- in Krakow streets were packed with Jewish synagogues.
Krakow was not destroyed.
And as a young kid, we saw a synagogue.
We saw a Star of David.
We couldn't get in there, but it was all Jewish-- no Jews left there.
There was one more house which kept Jews.
They took-- [NON-ENGLISH] 10.
Which actually they transferred my mother from the hospital to that place there, to [NON-ENGLISH] 10.
Like I said, a lot of the kids who didn't go to Zakopane, they arranged a place called, Rabka.
Rabka was a little bit closer to Krakow, and the air is a little bit different.
It's also in the country.
And they took a lot of children to Rabka from Krakow.
Now, when we got to Zakopane, for three days she wouldn't let us go outside to get to the air of Zakopane because-- not to get sick.
Because we were sick people.
We had-- first thing she did is to take everybody and see the-- we were on the third floor, on the second floor were the girls, the bottom floor was there for the schools.
But we were on the third floor-- everybody had to get undressed in the nude.
Yeah, Helena Kuchler
Lena Kuchler.
I would not spell it because I might not spell it the right way.
My impression-- she was a very strong woman.
She came into Zakopa-- to Krakow-- I met her in Krakow only twice, or three times.
I actually never talked to her in Krakow at all.
I met her.
I saw her coming in, but I never-- she never talked to us too much, to the bigger boys.
But when we came to Zakopane we talked to her a lot.
But she was a strong woman.
She was not a-- to the older boy she was not weak, she was not sitting down.
She was-- somebody had to organize.
She was running the show.
She ran all the thing is Zakopane.
There were other women.
There was a woman that she was very-- familiar with me.
Edith was very nice to me.
She says how to romance the girls, and how to behave, not to be so rough, and not to hit, and to carry with her little finger.
All kinds of things.
But she was occupied.
Maybe, I'll say that Helena Kuchler was more connected to the younger.
We had children of seven, and eight, and nine years old.
And maybe to them she was more connected to us. 16-year-old boys, 17-year-old boys, 15-year-old boys-- we went through a lot.
We knew how to take care of ourselves, we knew how-- before we got the guns, and all that.
But we knew how to look after ourselves.
The only thing is, when we came to Zakopane, like I said, we took off all our clothes.
We went in to wash.
Lots of soap, and lots of washing.
They cut our hair off, and-- just at towel around us, and to go into a room, which in the room was four, or six, to eight guys.
Nice white sheets with pillowcases.
It was something unbelievable.
And we went down to eat.
First thing, all the clothes were-- we never saw that again.
And we got clothes.
In Zakopane, like you say, from outside-- I knew that-- we got help from Switzerland, we got help from the Polish government.
Like she said, you didn't-- we did get, from the Polish government, help.
The Polish government wanted to make a big splash about supporting Jewish refug-- orphans.
Because that was an orphanage.
There were no fathers or mothers around.
Nobody-- there was one woman, a professor woman, that she had a son there.
She was a teacher.
She taught us.
I have a picture of her.
Now, from the-- and there was another woman.
She was teaching to dance and to-- for exercise, and all kinds of gymnastics, and all that.
But after three days we went outside.
We didn't-- we made trips into the mountains, we made trips around--
The villa was gorgeous.
The villa was unbelievable.
We had such beautiful grounds.
We had a beautiful building.
We had-- the dining room was a huge dining room.
We had a piano in the dining room.
Oh, yeah, Kuchler could play piano.
She had friends coming in from Warsaw.
We used to have kids coming in and bringing them from Warsaw, too.
That's why I say there were lots of children.
There were children coming in from Warsaw.
There were children coming in from Lodz-- not only from Krakow now.
How did she get this villa?
Who provided-- That's exactly what I said, she did not organize it all by herself.
But I'm sure that it was organized by the Jewish committees, with Jewish people, that she knew.
And she was in charge of everything.
Yeah, she was in charge.
But, like I say, the Polish people in Krakow did not like a Jewish villa to be there.
We were abused by Poles.
We went alone in the city.
We, as the boys, we didn't care.
We hit back.
No, no.
In Zakopane.
In Zakopane.
If we went alone-- if we went alone to a movie, or anything like that, we used to have to fight.
The Polish guys didn't like [NON-ENGLISH].
And if there was a Jew-- they beat him up, and all that-- we fought back.
The big ones-- we were fighting.
The smaller ones, she wouldn't let go.
So we used to fight a lot of times.
She used to scream at us a lot of times because we used to fight back.
The only thing, as I say-- food.
When we got the food in Zakopane, we couldn't stop eating.
There was a lot of food.
But she got help from Switzerland.
She got help-- they say at the end, when I was there, that she got from the Jewish Congress in the United States, too.
But when she took away the children from there, they said that the Polish government was very furious because she ran out from Poland.
And to the Polish government that was a slap in the face.
Oh, yes.
There was a lot of them that went-- you know, we had school in the villa.
I went to school in there.
I was-- we weren't there sitting idle.
We were always learning.
But the ones who were getting a little bit up, and they had to be integrated in the Polish school.
But the incident, the fighting in the school, I was not there already.
When they started to go to school, somebody came to Zakopane, and by accident he said that my brother died in Krakow.
So I left Zakopane.
But before I left Zakopane, they wanted to kill us because at that time I had a machine gun on me.
We all had a big, huge gun on top of the roof.
They came with horses, and they wanted to kill us.
And we start shooting.
And they didn't know that we had guns.
She went to the Polish government and they gave her.
The Polish government brought them from the army.
And they had a few of the Polish-- I think-- men also that they came in and-- they showed me how to use it, but I knew how use it.
But I didn't know this was a machine gun of 72 bullets.
So to carry around with me-- and they came one night, and we were running around.
But from then on we used to already stay guard.
They used to put out guards at night to go around in circle.
Every two hours we used to change.
Only the big ones. 20 guys, 30 guys.
The girls also shot with a pistol.
The Polish.
Polish-- you see, in Poland they called it [POLISH].
It was the Polish National Army.
They wanted to liberate Poland from the Russian, or from Communism, or from whatever they wanted.
But they wanted to have no Jews in Poland.
They didn't attack.
One evening a woman came rushing in, a Polish woman, and warned us that they're coming.
We covered the gates, and we got out, and we were ready.
As soon as they came up to us, before they even start shooting or anything like-- they didn't even put one shot-- we shot.
We were putting out the fire.
And we didn't give them a chance to open fire.
So there was no killing, nobody-- anything done to us.
And they took away from there.
And that incident that was at the time, and after that I left.
I left maybe a week or two weeks later.
When I heard my brother died in Krakow-- so I left.
I would say from all the time-- from the-- May of [INAUDIBLE] came to Krakow, to Zakopane, I was in there between there about a year. '45, '46.
About a year.
There was always talk of Palestine, but not the way it happened because we didn't-- first thing, I didn't know, but it was always talked.
What is going to be with us, we knew we can't remain there.
But there was no-- there was always people coming.
There was a professor to me-- all kinds of professors used to come.
From all over people used to come to visit.
A professor talked to me, and he said to me-- and I always remember that-- he says, before you say it, it's yours, after you say it, it's somebody else's.
And I remember that.
So you see, a lot of times I think about it.
You say something-- you would like to pull it back, but it doesn't go back.
So he said-- all things.
A lot of people came to visit us,
No religious, whatsoever.
There was no prayers and religions in Zakopane.
We did not convert back to Judaism.
I did not.
None of the boys wanted to.
No Jewish religion was done.
There was no lighting of candles, there was no religion whatsoever.
My religion was very unreligion.
Whatever happened-- and what the rabbi said, that [NON-ENGLISH], in the name of God we're dying, I don't believe it till this day.
If people would-- pick up something, hit back, I think there would be many, many more left alive.
And this is my idea.
Not to go like sheep.
Not to-- to fight back.
It's all right.
They say, they have respect, or they have fear for the guy who hits back.
And it's true.
I learned it when I was small.
When I hit that guy, he was my friend afterwards.
I was only six or seven at that time.
So my religion-- but what it is, when I got back to Krakow, and my brother-- my mother told me-- my brother died because he was dying and he needed a transfusion.
Nobody would give a transfusion.
Nobody would give him blood.
My mother gave him a few times and the doctors say, no more.
You'll die with him.
And they stopped taking from my mother, too, and he died.
Where was he when he died?
He died on [NON-ENGLISH] 38, in that hospital.
Yeah, he died on [NON-ENGLISH] 38.
I went back because I didn't see him.
He was dead already and everything else.
My mother wanted-- I should say Kaddish for my brother.
So in those days-- was already late.
This was in '46.
And in '46 there was lots of kibbutzim in Krakow.
In different places, in Jewish places, there was kibbutzim.
And she wanted me to join a religious kibbutz.
And I joined a religious kibbutz to listen to my mother, to say Kaddish for my brother.
So I went into a Mizrahi kibbutz.
I came to the kibbutz with a few suitcases of clothes, one of the best clothes there is.
In Zakopane they gave us clothes.
They brought clothes from all over just to give to the children.
From where they got it?
I don't know, but they was coming clothes-- they came from the United States, or from Switzerland, or from where-- they came clothes.
I had coats, I had-- When I came into the kibbutz, the kibbutz says, everything belongs to the kibbutz.
They took away all my clothes, and I didn't have anything to wear.
And I saw them wearing mine, and I said, where is mine?
I had pajamas, I had all-- it's collective.
That I didn't like already.
But the idea why I went to the kibbutz is to leave for Palestine.
The kibbutz was organized, and the idea-- I says, I'm coming in, but my mother goes with me.
First they argued with me-- I said, if you argue with me I'm not staying.
I'm leaving.
So they promised to take my mother.
I wasn't there very long.
We left Krakow.
We went to the German border, on the East German border between Poland and East.
And we went there.
We crossed at night.
We were, I think, 25 or 30 people in the kibbutz at that time.
We crossed through a river, and we crossed over.
We crossed over, we went over to our station where we're going to take the train to Berlin.
Somebody came over and he says, [NON-ENGLISH] and this and that, and he starts talking in Yiddish to us, and everything else.
Where are you going?
We're going to Berlin.
It took him a few minutes-- he disappeared and all of a sudden the German police came and arrested us.
And they put us in jail.
All of you?
Everybody?
Everybody.
They put me and my mother in a cell.
And the food was horrible.
That was just very bad.
The only time they let us out just for a little bit of exercise in the yard, and then they put us back in.
But no questions as-- we're in the yard, we used to ask, did they ask you something-- nobody was asked a question.
And we didn't see Russian, we saw German police.
After-- it was 10 days, or 14 days, we were there.
They took us back across the border into Poland and let us go.
Without saying a word.
Two days later we heard there's a boat going on the German side of the British.
There's a port there, [NON-ENGLISH].
No, Szczecin.
Szczecin, a Polish port, and we took-- 5,000 Germans, and 25 or 30 Jews went out there, and we said-- we went on the port, and we crossed over the Baltic Sea into Lubeck, to Germany.
But there we wind up in a camp between the Germans.
But we-- there we had no [INAUDIBLE], too.
The British-- the British would send us back.
All of a sudden came somebody in a British uniform with a Magen David-- first time we saw-- it was one of the Jewish brigades.
And he took us out of there.
He snuck us out of there, and he says quietly, you come with me.
He took us into a room, and we were all in one room.
We stayed there until late at night, and there was a train going.
And we left there.
We went to-- on our way to Munchen.
Munchen was on the American side.
Because Germany was divided into four.
So we went to Munchen.
In Munchen, they split me with my mother up.
Who is they at this time?
The kibbutz.
The Russian kibbutz and all that, and they said, you come with us, and your mother's going to go somewhere else.
So I went with them.
I said, that's all right.
They don't know what it's going to be.
So I went with the kibbutz to a place called Pocking.
I stayed there a few weeks, and I said, I don't want to be here.
Pocking is just outside of Munchen, but about an hour train ride, or something like that.
There was a Russian kibbutz.
There was somebody in charge of the kibbutz.
I don't remember his name because I couldn't care less.
Because the idea is-- first thing you see-- it was supposed to be a religious kibbutz.
They took everything away from me, which I didn't have nothing, and he was wearing my pajamas, he was wearing my shirt, and I didn't have-- and that was supposed to be collective.
He can wear it, I can't wear it-- I brought it.
And working-- I worked like a horse.
Oh, yeah--
What kind of work?
We used to make cigarettes.
We used to get the thing-- the-- without the tobacco on them.
And they we used pinch cigarettes and tobacco inside.
And we used to work there for hours to make.
And all different things to-- everything there.
And if you knew you have to go to the kitchen, you work.
And you have to peel potatoes-- everything has to be done.
The laundry-- I was never a lazy fellow.
Always did-- but the idea is-- I was not treated right.
OK.
